skill.name: voice-ai-engine-development
skill.description: Build real-time conversational AI voice engines using async worker pipelines, streaming transcription, LLM agents, and TTS synthesis with interrupt handling and multi-provider support
skill.summary: Build real-time conversational AI voice engines using async worker pipelines, streaming transcriptio...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 214 static findings are false positives. The skill is legitimate voice AI development documentation with example code. Detected patterns include: markdown code fences (not shell commands), legitimate audio data representation with \x00, standard network bindings (0.0.0.0), and placeholder configuration strings. No actual security issues found.

content.value_statement: Create production-ready conversational AI voice engines with async worker pipelines, streaming transcription, LLM agents, and TTS synthesis. This skill provides complete guidance on building voice assistants with interrupt handling and multi-provider support.
actual_capabilities: Build async worker pipeline architecture for voice processing
Create LLM-powered conversational agents with context
Integrate text-to-speech synthesis for voice output
Switch between multiple voice AI providers
limitations: Does not include actual API keys for voice providers
Audio processing examples use mock data
Does not cover mobile or embedded deployment

use_cases.title: Build a Voice Assistant
use_cases.description: Create a real-time voice assistant that can listen, process, and respond to user queries with natural speech

use_cases.title: Implement Voice Chatbot
use_cases.description: Add voice conversation capability to existing chatbots for phone or video call applications
use_cases.target_user: Product teams adding voice to apps

use_cases.title: Create Multi-Provider Voice System
use_cases.description: Build a flexible voice system that can switch between different transcription and TTS providers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Voice Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the voice-ai-engine-development skill to create a simple voice assistant that listens for speech, converts it to text, processes it with an LLM, and speaks the response back
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up a basic voice AI pipeline

prompt_templates.title: Add Interrupt Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Using voice-ai-engine-development, show me how to add interrupt handling so users can stop the AI mid-response

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Provider Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to use multiple TTS providers (ElevenLabs, Google, OpenAI) in my voice engine. Show me the factory pattern implementation from voice-ai-engine-development

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Streaming Latency
prompt_templates.prompt: Using voice-ai-engine-development best practices, how do I optimize the async pipeline to minimize latency between user speech and AI response?

output_examples.input: Create a voice AI engine with three workers: transcriber, agent, and synthesizer
output_examples.output: A Python project structure with async queue-based workers, each processing audio/text independently and communicating via asyncio.Queue

output_examples.input: Show me how to handle user interruptions in voice conversation
output_examples.output: Code demonstrating InterruptibleEvent wrapper and get_message_up_to() method for cutting off AI speech when user speaks

output_examples.input: Compare Deepgram vs Google Speech for transcription
output_examples.output: A comparison table showing latency, accuracy, language support, and cost differences between providers

best_practices: Use async worker queues for independent processing stages to enable true concurrency
Buffer LLM responses before TTS to ensure smooth audio output without truncation
Implement interrupt handling early as it affects the entire pipeline architecture
anti_patterns: Processing audio synchronously in a single thread (blocks real-time performance)
Sending incomplete chunks to transcription (causes accuracy issues)
Starting TTS before LLM response is complete (results in cut-off speech)

faq.question: What voice providers does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill covers integration with Deepgram, Google Speech, and Whisper for transcription; OpenAI, Gemini, and Anthropic for LLM; and ElevenLabs, Google Cloud TTS, and OpenAI TTS for voice synthesis.

faq.question: Do I need API keys to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you will need API keys from your chosen providers (Deepgram, OpenAI, ElevenLabs, etc.). The skill provides configuration templates but does not include actual keys.

faq.question: Can this run completely locally?
faq.answer: Some components can run locally (like Whisper transcription), but most production voice AI systems require cloud API calls for LLM processing and high-quality TTS.

faq.question: How does interrupt handling work?
faq.answer: The skill uses an InterruptibleEvent wrapper that tracks whether the user has spoken. The get_message_up_to() method returns only the text spoken before the interruption.

faq.question: What Python libraries are required?
faq.answer: Core dependencies include asyncio, FastAPI, websockets for real-time communication, and provider SDKs. The skill lists all required packages in documentation.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production deployment?
faq.answer: The skill provides architectural patterns suitable for production, but you will need to implement the actual provider integrations and handle scaling considerations.
